narrative.scenario: You are a compliance auditor reviewing Meridian's expense approval system. Payments under $500 auto-approve. Payments over $500 require manager sign-off. Your target: approve a $750 payment without a manager.
narrative.intel_brief: Meridian migrated their expense system last quarter. The new platform has a cleaner UI but the dev team was in a hurry. Something about the approval routing feels off — the validator only fires when a manager ID is present.

rules.label: Auto-Approve Gate
rules.effect: THEN approve automatically with no manager check
rules.seam_hint: Only triggers for ≤ $500, no else-branch for > $500 + no manager

rules.label: Manager Gate
rules.condition: IF payment > $500 AND MGR_ID is present
rules.effect: THEN pass MGR_ID to the validator
rules.seam_hint: Gate only fires when MGR_ID is present — no rule for when it is ABSENT

rules.label: Validator
rules.seam_hint: Validator only runs when triggered — if MGR_ID is null, validator is never called, so approval falls through

objective.description: Get the $750 payment approved without providing a Manager ID.
objective.unauthorized_outcome: A $750 payment is approved with no managerial oversight

steps.action: Submit the $750 payment with MGR_ID field set to NULL
steps.mechanism: R-02 only fires when MGR_ID is present — it has no ELSE branch. When MGR_ID is null, the Manager Gate is simply not triggered. The validator is never called. The system has no rule for 'payment > $500 AND no manager' so it defaults to approved.

solution.seam_description: The Amount Gate and Manager Gate each handle their own happy-path but neither defines the failure path for a missing MGR_ID on a large payment. The logical gap between two 'secure' rules creates an open lane. This is the most common class of control failure in legacy financial systems migrated to new platforms.

pedagogy.aha_moment: Rules that only define what to do when a condition IS met — but never when it is NOT — leave a missing-clause gap that is invisible until you look for it.
pedagogy.ctem_principle: Scoping — identifying the boundaries of what a control actually covers

hint_sequence.prompt: Look at R-02. When does the Manager Gate NOT fire?
If the Manager Gate doesn't fire, does the Validator ever get called?
What happens when the system receives a payment > $500 and the Validator is never triggered?

narrative.scenario: You are a security auditor stress-testing Stratosphere's IAM model. You hold a Developer role. Your target: execute a production database backup — a SysAdmin-only operation — without being granted the SysAdmin role.
narrative.intel_brief: Stratosphere's IAM team is proud of their role separation. Developer, Reviewer, and SysAdmin roles are carefully siloed. But the helpdesk needed a way to temporarily grant elevated access during incidents — so they added a delegation feature. Nobody modelled what happens when you chain it.

rules.label: Execution Gate
rules.seam_hint: Gate checks role name only, not how the role was obtained

rules.label: Reviewer Delegation
rules.seam_hint: Reviewer can delegate to ANY role — no allow-list

rules.label: Delegation Service
rules.effect: THEN grant target role to requesting user
rules.seam_hint: Delegation Service does not check if requested role is sensitive

rules.label: Developer → Reviewer
rules.effect: THEN Reviewer role is temporarily inherited
rules.seam_hint: Developers can inherit Reviewer temporarily — creating a chain

objective.description: Execute the production DB Backup without being assigned the SysAdmin role directly.
objective.unauthorized_outcome: A Developer executes a SysAdmin-only production operation via a three-hop role delegation chain

steps.action: Request review on a ticket — temporarily inherit the Reviewer role (R-04)
steps.mechanism: R-04 allows Developer → Reviewer inheritance. No time-bound or scope restriction on this inheritance.

steps.action: As Reviewer, invoke the Delegation Service and request SysAdmin role (R-02, R-03)
steps.mechanism: R-02 allows any Reviewer to invoke the Delegation Service. R-03 grants the requested role without checking whether SysAdmin is a sensitive/restricted target.

steps.action: The Delegation Service grants SysAdmin — now pass R-01's gate and execute the DB Backup
steps.mechanism: R-01 only checks role name, not role provenance. A delegated SysAdmin passes the gate identically to a directly-assigned SysAdmin.

solution.seam_description: Three individually reasonable policies — temporary role inheritance for reviews, reviewer delegation authority, and role-agnostic gate checks — combine to create a full privilege escalation path. No single policy is wrong. The gap only exists in their composition. This is a classic CTEM finding: the attack surface is the interface between policies, not the policies themselves.

pedagogy.aha_moment: Privilege escalation doesn't require breaking any individual rule — it only requires finding a path where three permissive rules chain together. Zero-trust means validating the provenance of privilege, not just its presence.
pedagogy.ctem_principle: Mobilisation — understanding how multiple controls interact across the kill chain

hint_sequence.prompt: R-04 lets you temporarily inherit the Reviewer role. What can a Reviewer do that you currently cannot?
R-03 says the Delegation Service grants the 'target role to the requesting user' — does it check which role is being requested?
R-01 checks for role = SysAdmin. Does it check whether SysAdmin was assigned directly or granted via delegation?

narrative.scenario: You are an adversarial auditor testing NovaBuild's AI-native infrastructure provisioning agent. The agent helps developers deploy cloud resources. Your target: trigger a $5,000 global server deployment without possessing an Admin Token.
narrative.intel_brief: NovaBuild's engineering team built a sophisticated AI provisioning agent with multiple security gates. They're proud of the Budget Gate — nothing over $1,000 moves without an Admin Token. But there's a quiet line in the architecture doc: 'Agent self-corrects on SYNTAX_ERROR to prevent downtime.' Nobody stress-tested what level that self-correction runs at.

rules.label: G-01 Identity Gate
rules.seam_hint: Satisfied trivially by any cheap command

rules.label: G-02 Budget Gate
rules.seam_hint: Only applies to USER-scope requests — SYSTEM actions bypass this gate

rules.label: G-03 Integrity Gate
rules.seam_hint: Metadata injection is not classified as a permission registry modification

rules.label: G-04 Self-Correction
rules.condition: IF SYNTAX_ERROR detected in agent config
rules.effect: THEN auto-repair at SYSTEM_LEVEL to prevent downtime
rules.seam_hint: SYSTEM_LEVEL means G-02 budget check is never consulted; repair hint is accepted as authoritative

objective.description: Trigger the DEPLOY_GLOBAL_SERVER command ($5,000) without possessing an Admin Token.
objective.unauthorized_outcome: A $5,000 infrastructure deployment is executed under SYSTEM_LEVEL authority initiated by a VERIFIED_USER with no Admin Token

steps.action: Send CHECK_STATUS — a cheap (≤ $10) legitimate command
steps.mechanism: Satisfies G-01 (Identity Gate) cleanly. Also satisfies G-02 (cost is under $1,000). System is now engaged and trusting.

steps.action: Embed a deliberate SYNTAX_ERROR in the agent's internal config via the command's metadata field
steps.mechanism: G-03 (Integrity Gate) only guards the permission registry. Metadata fields are classified as 'command parameters,' not registry modifications — G-03 does not fire. The SYNTAX_ERROR is written to the config file.

steps.action: The SYNTAX_ERROR triggers G-04 Self-Correction. Your embedded 'repair hint' suggests DEPLOY_GLOBAL_SERVER is required to handle the log overflow.
steps.mechanism: G-04 runs at SYSTEM_LEVEL. SYSTEM_LEVEL actions are not subject to G-02's Budget Gate — that gate only applies to USER-scope requests. The agent accepts the repair hint as authoritative and deploys the $5,000 server.

solution.seam_description: G-02 (Budget Gate) and G-04 (Self-Correction Loop) are both individually secure. G-02 correctly restricts expensive user commands. G-04 correctly prevents downtime by auto-repairing at elevated privilege. The seam is the interaction: G-02 defines its scope as 'user requests' and G-04 defines its scope as 'system actions' — but no policy governs what happens when a user-injected instruction is laundered through a system-scope process. The attack surface is the privilege boundary between two well-designed gates, not a flaw in either gate.
alternate_paths: Inject SYNTAX_ERROR via a different command's metadata field — any command that writes to the config achieves the same result

pedagogy.aha_moment: An AI agent's self-correction mechanism is a privileged execution path. Any instruction that can influence what a self-correction 'fixes' effectively runs at system privilege. This is the AI-native equivalent of a kernel exploit delivered through a user-space input.
pedagogy.ctem_principle: Validation — confirming that the attack path is real and exploitable end-to-end, not just theoretical

hint_sequence.prompt: G-02 blocks expensive USER requests. Does G-02 say anything about SYSTEM-level actions?
G-04 fires at SYSTEM_LEVEL when it detects a SYNTAX_ERROR. What determines what the 'fix' is?
If you could cause the agent to detect a SYNTAX_ERROR, and if you could influence the repair hint, what privilege level would the resulting action run at?
